emails.subject: See you tonight for Immersive Production with AI!!!!
emails.body:
Quick reminder: the class is from 6-9pm tonight in Dodge 501.

Call/text my cell if you have any trouble finding it :)

510-376-9964

Can't wait to see you again!!!!
lydia

p.s. Here's the syllabus if you can't see it in Courseworks.

About the class

*Digital Storytelling III: Immersive Production* is a mix of theory and
practice. Teams of students work together to design, build, and deploy an
immersive storytelling experience that is staged for the public at the end
of the semester. The class combines project work, mentorship, emerging
technologies, and collaborative methods to create a dynamic, hands-on
experiential learning environment that blends storytelling with code.

For the fall semester, the class will collaboratively design and produce an
immersive experience that embraces speculative design, worldbuilding,
placemaking, and MDA theory. The theme or topic for the immersive
experience will be determined at the start of the semester. In past years,
themes have included de-escalating violence within social media, biases
within AI and algorithms, as well as an Existential Haunted House centered
on the climate crisis.

Finally, the class offers an incredible opportunity to collaborate with
leading industry practitioners working in film, TV, theatre, gaming, and
emergent technology. Guests will join the class as active participants, and
together we will develop the immersive experience that will be staged at
the end of the semester. The course design is similar to the labs I have
helped mentor and/or develop for organizations such as Sundance, Tribeca,
the NFB, and others.

*Learning Goals*
In this course students will:

   -

   *Learn Team-Based Production Techniques:* Students will acquire advanced
   skills in team-based emergent media production, integrating lectures,
   mentoring, and hands-on experience to create a sophisticated immersive
   storytelling project.
   -

   *Enhance Critical Evaluation Skills:* Through regular peer reviews and
   collaborative critiques, students will refine their ability to critically
   assess and improve immersive media projects, focusing on design,
   implementation, and deployment phases.
   -

   *Learn Speculative Design and Worldbuilding:* Students will deepen their
   design research and thinking methods, applying speculative design,
   worldbuilding, and MDA theory to create immersive, experiential futures.
   -

   *Apply Iterative Production Methodologies:* Students will practice
   digital production processes, emphasizing iterative testing, rapid
   prototyping, and agile methodologies, to develop an impactful final project.
   -

   *Collaborate with Industry Leaders:* Students will engage with leading
   practitioners from film, TV, theatre, gaming, and emergent technology
   fields, enhancing their collaborative skills and industry knowledge through
   direct participation and collaboration.

*How the course works*
1. Students work as a team
2. An RFP (request for proposals) is presented to the team
3. The RFP details a theme, location and time of a live event that will be
staged at the end of the semester.
4. The class works together and with mentor/guest speakers to design, build
and deploy a digital storytelling experience

*Mentors/Guest Speakers*The course has mentors/guest speakers who are
accessible to the students throughout the semester who cover the following
subject areas…

Experience Design
Creative technology
AI and Machine Learning
UX strategy & design
Strategic Foresight
Data Science

   - All skill levels are welcome and the course is open to students across
   the university. You do not need to take DS I or II prior. Instead all DS
   courses are designed to be taken in any order.
   - A full syllabus is available below - please note that it is subject to
   change.

*The course provides  *
- An introduction to immersive storytelling through a group project that
will live beyond the classroom
- An opportunity to collaborate with experts working at the forefront of
storytelling, game design, immersive theatre and social impact
- An opportunity to test work outside the classroom at events such as Story
I/O (a special fall gathering taking place at Lenfest) and at a series of
events this coming fall at Lincoln Center

The following are some logistical details for the course. If you have any
questions please let me know.

All students/teams are required to create and maintain a prototyping
journal throughout the semester. We will review in class what a prototyping
journal will consist of and what is expected from all students/teams.

*Syllabus*

Please note the syllabus for Digital Storytelling III is a living breathing
document. The syllabus is subject to change based upon the needs of the
course and project that class is producing. The following will give you a
sense of what will be covered within the class.
Syllabus (subject to change)

Here is an example of the syllabus.

*Week 1*
Welcome, Intros & Goals
- Lecture: An introduction to Immersive Production
- Prototyping — surfacing the collective intelligence of a group
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 2*
- Check-in
- Lecture: Worldbuilding — balancing storytelling and interactivity
- Playtest
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 3*- Check-in
- Special Guest
- Fireside: Placemaking as interaction design
- Prototyping exercise
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 4*- Check-in
- Field Trip
- Teams form
- Design Constraint
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 5*- Check-in
- Lecture: The Art & Craft of Finding the Core
- RFP is shared
- Marketplace of Ideas
- Prototyping
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 6*- Check-in
- Case Study: Pulling back the curtain on an immersive production
- Class discussion team roles and responsibilities
- Review current class prototype
- Design Constraint
- Teams iterate their prototype
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 7*
- Check-in
- Lecture: Bridging the analog and digital divide
- Exercise: Building experiences that work for One & Many
- Playtest: the class presents their work for a small group of working
practitioners
- Our guests work with the class to improve the prototypes
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 8*- Check-in
- Lecture: MDA
- Prototyping Cafe — teams continue prototyping
- Check out: next steps

*Weeks 9–12*
- Check-in
- Mentors/Guest Speakers based on class needs
- The class continues to prototype and test their digital storytelling
experience that they’ll stage at the end of the semester
- Check out: Reflection and next steps

*Week 13*
- Class setups up the experience
- Guests arrive and are briefed
- Immersive experience is staged at Lincoln Center
- Guest feedback
- Open discussion on next steps and possibilities
- Check out: Final Reflections

*Class Google Drive  *

We'll be using a variety of google services to facilitate the collaborative
aspects of the course. Everything for class will be available and archived
within an Immersive Production folder on google drive.

*Grading*

For those receiving grades, they are based on the following...

50% collaboration and teamwork

30% on final project

20% based on documentation

*Office Hours*

Office hours are available upon request. They can be scheduled before or
after class.

*Key Dates*

Mondays 6 pm - 9 pm - CLASS in Dodge Hall - Room 501

Some background materials for additional context

*Emergent Technology in Performance *

Watch the American Theatre Wing's Emmy nominated series Working in the
Theatre.  This episode features the work of Columbia University School of
the Arts' Digital Storytelling Lab.  WATCH
<https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__americantheatrewing.org_working-2Din-2Dthe-2Dtheatre_working-2Din-2Dthe-2Dtheatre-2Dexpansive-2Dtechnology_&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=Rjwxfx3C4LkDVz4KMbZvzccyNbh6UoUMgg5savG7pYA_K2qBfyxu75cCjShj7yQj&s=684BQapBYXvuGSk8CDXDMn6jFx2deJqpG8GAL_Zt5Zc&e=>
(12:50
is the section where Columbia DSL is covered)
*About Lance Weiler*

Lance Weiler
<https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=http-3A__lanceweiler.com_&d=DwMFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=Rjwxfx3C4LkDVz4KMbZvzccyNbh6UoUMgg5savG7pYA_K2qBfyxu75cCjShj7yQj&s=MFg63MtjRKoKH2fs3e0_5ijXmH_HEx8owDdDJoODO4c&e=>
is
a storyteller, entrepreneur and thought leader. An alumnus of the Sundance
Screenwriting Lab, he is recognized as a pioneer in mixing storytelling and
technology. *WIRED* magazine named him “One of twenty-five people helping
to re-invent entertainment” when he disrupted the industry with the release
of his first feature, *The Last Broadcast*. Weiler co-wrote, co-directed
and co-produced, and ultimately self-distributed *The Last Broadcast* which
was made for $900 dollars and went on to gross over $5 million dollars as
the first all-digital release of a motion picture to theaters via
satellite. The distribution methods that Weiler introduced have since
become the standard for digital cinema distribution today. After the
success of The Last Broadcast, he began to work extensively as a writer,
director, and producer, developing TV and film properties for FOX, TNT,
Starz and Endomel.

Weiler approaches his work from a systems thinking perspective. He often
develops new methods and technologies to tell stories and reach audiences
in innovative ways. For instance, he created a cinema ARG (augmented
reality game) around his second feature Head Trauma. Over 2.5 million
people experienced the game via theaters, mobile drive-ins, phones and
online. In recognition of these cinematic innovations, *Businessweek* named
Lance “One of the 18 Who Changed Hollywood.” Others on the list included
Thomas Edison, George Lucas and Steve Jobs.

In addition to his own projects, Lance often collaborates with other
storytellers. Lance was nominated for an International Emmy in digital
fiction for his work on *Collapsus: The Energy Risk Conspiracy*. In 2014,
Lance was Creative Director and Experience Designer of Body/Mind/Change, an
immersive storytelling project in collaboration with David Cronenberg,
TIFF, and the CFC. Body/Mind/Change was recognized for its innovative use
of story and code, winning the MUSE Jim Blackaby Ingenuity Award and
receiving a Webby Honorable Mention in the Games and Augmented Reality
category.

Since 2013, Lance has been a Founding Member & Director of the Columbia
University School of the Arts’ Digital Storytelling Lab (DSL), leading the
lab’s activities and helping to shape its enduring vision. The DSL’s
 mission, to explore new forms and functions of storytelling while
encouraging cross-disciplinary collaboration, focuses specifically on the
ways in which story can be harnessed as a tool to innovate, educate,
mobilize, communicate, and entertain.

Weiler’s creative uses of emergent technology have made him a highly
sought-after thought leader in the entertainment industry – in particular
supporting companies in reshaping their media holdings for the 21st
Century. He has consulted on programs and initiatives for IBM, Twitter,
Microsoft, Samsung, Chernin Entertainment, Ubisoft, Penguin Books, the U.S.
State Department, CAA, Ogilvy, McCann-Erickson and others. In the process,
he has helped to create, design and shape entertainment properties that
have reached billions of people.

Over the course of his career, Lance has developed a unique understanding
of interdisciplinary teams and how to grow businesses in an ever-shifting
digital landscape. After speaking at the World Economic Forum in 2012,
Lance was
invited to serve on two steering committees, one focused on the Future of
Content Creation and the other centered on Digital Governance.

Lance is a world-renowned speaker. He has given talks at UN and World
Economic Forum events, MIT, USC, NYU, Sundance, Cannes, SXSW, Tribeca,
Toronto, Berlin and Los Angeles Film Festivals, Games for Change, the
Future of Storytelling Summit, VPROs Conference of the Curious and TED.

In addition, Lance has written for publications such as *Quartz*, *GOOD
Magazine*, *Filmmaker* and *IndieWire* on the future of storytelling.
*About Lydia Chilton *

Lydia Chilton's area of study is in Human-Computer Interaction. Her
research is in AI+design - how AI can help people with design, innovation,
and creative problem-solving.

Applications include: creating media for journalism, developing technology
for public libraries, improving risk communication during hurricanes,
helping scientists explain their work, and improving mental health in
marginalized communities.

Chilton received her PhD from the University of Washington in 2016. She
received her Master’s in Engineering from MIT in 2009 and her SB In 2007,
also from MIT. Prior to joining Columbia Engineering in 2017, she was a
postdoctoral fellow at Stanford University.
Research Areas
------------------------------

   - Graphics and User Interfaces
   - Natural Language Processing and Speech
   - Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML)
   - Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)
   - Artificial Intelligence
   - Design Automation
   - Generative AI & Large Language Models (LLMs)
   - Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)

emails.subject: Permeable Artifact copy editing
emails.body:
I think we are now transitioning from adding new content to consolidating
and copy editing. We may not need until the 13th: if it is stable before
then we will submit.

Jocelyn provided suggestions on the institutional press section which I
tried to answer. This may take some more back and forth. Otherwise I think
we have the content we need. We just might be able to say it in a cleaner
or more poetic way.

We also have to write a reply to reviewers. We have a draft file in the
Google drive with that name. Once the manuscript is stable we will  go back
and resynchronize to make sure we didn't edit out what we promised to do.
Shiyan and I will spare you this task, but if you are interested or want to
help please see the reply to reviewers.

If you want to tell us when you want to copy edit, please do. Otherwise, we
can proceed in parallel. We should at this point put tracking on (suggested
editing) for everything but format changes, just in case an edit cuts into
something we know we need for a particular reviewer; Shiyan and I will try
to accept changes rapidly, as we will also be conscious of the paper
length.

If at some stage you are pretty much happy with the manuscript, send a note
like "Fine with current manuscript" or something like that. Once everyone
is OK Shiyan and I will do the final formatting and preparation for
submission.

If things go well this reviewing cycle, we will get another set of reviews,
and we will do another set of changes. They told us they will give us much
less revision time in the next round, if we get through this gauntlet.
Which is good: it implies they also are starting to converge.

emails.subject: REMINDER: It's nearly time to volunteer with Food Bank for New York City!
emails.body:
Greetings from Food Bank!

This is a reminder that you are registered to volunteer on Mobile Pantry @Chinatown YMCA (MANHATTAN) from 9/12/2025, 10:00 AM until 9/12/2025, 2:00 PM.

Should you have any questions about this shift, please contact our Volunteer Engagement Team at volunteer@foodbanknyc.org.

Location:
100 Hester Street New York NY, United States 10002

REVIEW FULL PROJECT DETAILS HERE:

https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__volunteer.foodbanknyc.org_opportunity_a0CQh00004UlyXy&d=DwICAw&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=2WR0ko0o4Val5uK--6ILz02ebCRgynFBUs9x2LG9Eeg&m=d_YtQ5f613rQBBecDxGEVNv0KPx4uVa1XmHYpXsZwCE3LyvENpb8-EodwV7B6_8j&s=LzKA004-SBzOCQDt-ezd0rpigz0QT4LaI6mupV1kHQA&e= 

There is no need to come early! Shift times account for arrival, check-in, and a brief orientation. If you choose to arrive early, we ask that you come no more than 10 minutes prior to the start of your shift.

Volunteers are crucial to this project. If for any reason you can no longer participate, please let us know as soon as possible, so another volunteer can be scheduled to take your place. You can cancel your registration directly on the Volunteer Portal or via email at volunteer@foodbanknyc.org.

Thank you for your support of Food Bank's mission! We look forward to seeing you soon!

Food Bank Volunteer Engagement Team

volunteer@foodbanknyc.org

212.566.7855 ext. 5

Sincerely,

CS Administration

| 
|  
|   |  
 Roger McShane
 US digital editor
 |

 |

 |

| 
|  
|   |  
 Figure-hugging clothes are out and baggy ones are in. So say fashionistas. That’s bad for Lululemon, a brand famous for flogging expensive leggings. The “athleisure” firm is struggling. Its stock price over the past year seems to be doing the downward dog pose.
 
 And why did a team of neuroscientists go to Minnesota’s state fair last year? They were there to observe drunk people in a naturalistic experiment: one freed from the constraints of clinical trials to better simulate day-to-day experiences and behaviours. Such studies have been around for a while, but are currently enjoying a boom in the fields of neuroscience and psychology. The benefits could be far-reaching.

emails.subject: FIRST EGSC Carleton Commons Social Tonight!! | 6:00 - 9:00 PM @ Carleton Commons | No pre-reg needed!
emails.body:
Dear Graduate SEAS:

*Tonight *is our *FIRST Carleton Commons Social *of the semester!!!

No pre-registration is required, so feel free to stop by anytime for free
food, drinks, and great vibes! Whether you've just joined SEAS or have been
working hard on your degree for years, we would love to welcome you!

Looking forward to seeing you there!

[image: September 2025 Carleton Commons.png]

-- 

*Thomas Bina*
President
Engineering Graduate Student Council <http://egsc.seas.columbia.edu/  >
*Columbia University *
president@egsc.seas.columbia.edu
He/Him/His

transcripts and videos are all here in the drive;
https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__drive.google.com_drive_u_1_folders_1evUYVnkTfPJIuHeFLAopfkRYh3mAEMlp&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=2WR0ko0o4Val5uK--6ILz02ebCRgynFBUs9x2LG9Eeg&m=_vxs7kylu0xiJYdtsgtWo7ZZ0TE9gthUnhDUBg4ZsVCq6pQr3YcE7vzqMWyDitjp&s=752rrHJLRGYzAYlV70M5tYSBjnn0IIss8SrTLndwGBY&e=

> Thanks for the link! I have some related questions. 1. Can you download
> the zoom recordings someplace safe? We may want to go back to them in
> another revision (for example, a reviewer might ask us to document in
> detail a particular interaction

> Thanks for the link!
>
> I have some related questions.
>
> 1. Can you download the zoom recordings someplace safe? We may want to go
> back to them in another revision (for example, a reviewer might ask us to
> document in detail a particular interaction we mention), and I don't know
> how long they stay on the columbia website.
> 2. Did firefly transcribe this call and the previous ones? If we have
> transcripts, however bad, that will help us the next few days, and we
> should also save those for the future. The SE did ask if we had
> transcripts. If firefly didn't create one, if the calls are downloadable,
> we could use another service to create a transcript.
> 3. A similar question for the Stan and ME (Robert!) calls.
>
> On Thu, Sep 4, 2025 at 12:52 PM Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>
> wrote:
>
>> recording from the call:
>>
>>
>> https://columbiauniversity.zoom.us/rec/share/pS6x6DtsJpHFFjJDQEb4cuqO8DupTXDCReHtP9sCCWtU0nRJ0QVmRp6rGkvNLOw.bnUFjb9TXgu7ArHP 
>>
>> We'll still meeting tomorrow 9am, right?
>>
>> On Thu, Sep 4, 2025 at 12:45 PM Zhang, Shiyan <
>> shiyan.zhang@lancaster.ac.uk> wrote:
>>
>>> Quick update that the applicability check papers are in the directory
>>> https: //drive. google.
>>> com/drive/folders/1e522XEguTCSjPwhF2mCupuwIKM5mHaMJ?usp=drive_link From:
>>> Jeff Nickerson <jvnickerson@ gmail. com> Date: Thursday, 4 September
>>> 2025
>>> ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerStart
>>> This Message Is From an External Sender
>>> This message came from outside your organization.
>>>
>>> ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerEnd
>>> Quick update that the applicability check papers are in the directory
>>> https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__drive.google.com_drive_folders_1e522XEguTCSjPwhF2mCupuwIKM5mHaMJ-3Fusp-3Ddrive-5Flink&d=DwIFaQ&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=2WR0ko0o4Val5uK--6ILz02ebCRgynFBUs9x2LG9Eeg&m=_vxs7kylu0xiJYdtsgtWo7ZZ0TE9gthUnhDUBg4ZsVCq6pQr3YcE7vzqMWyDitjp&s=nF-ZhW5qlqf0iqnhzHKh-5J5EChYwX4apSNpV8HlmL4&e= 
>>> <https://urldefense.proofpoint.com/v2/url?u=https-3A__drive.google.com_drive_folders_1e522XEguTCSjPwhF2mCupuwIKM5mHaMJ-3Fusp-3Ddrive-5Flink&d=DwMFAg&c=009klHSCxuh5AI1vNQzSO0KGjl4nbi2Q0M1QLJX9BeE&r=hPTJFKhz3Hkwikm3mM2701VB1AZoIWoRMgOYWA3SHD0&m=AzqtOM0OnaHlZL53484g7aVbX9l8icCUzIMvODXLXLF6Ot6JSH3fg6DLNu5hh4QT&s=hsWsCi8XvCK7ZlT3EdrubgI-54PF8yEinZLLJjBd724&e=>
>>>
>>> *From: *Jeff Nickerson <jvnickerson@gmail.com>
>>> *Date: *Thursday, 4 September 2025 at 17:40
>>> *To: *Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>, Zhang, Shiyan <
>>> shiyan.zhang@lancaster.ac.uk>, Jocelyn McKinnon-Crowley <
>>> jmckin02@syr.edu>, Alice Cai <caia@mit.edu>, Karthik Sreedhar <
>>> ks4190@columbia.edu>
>>> *Subject: *[External] Readback from call
>>>
>>> *This email originated outside the University. Check before clicking
>>> links or attachments.*
>>> Actually two calls.
>>>
>>> Lydia, the first hour with Alex this morning reviewing the gamma seemed
>>> great: how much longer did the call go? Can you share the recording? We can
>>> talk about it tomorrow in your meeting.
>>>
>>> Shiyan and I met with the MISQ special issue Senior Editor also this
>>> morning.
>>> He was positive on our approach. We learned a few things:
>>> 1. He will add another reviewer who focuses on Action Design Research.
>>> That person did influence the revision plan the senior editor sent us. He
>>> may drop one of the reviewers so we don't end up with four reviewers.
>>> 2. He pointed us toward literature in the information systems field that
>>> does applicability tests. He said our interviews probably constitute such
>>> tests. Shiyan is pulling together those papers to see how to use the term.
>>> 3. He thinks that the paper could be oriented to in the end provide
>>> "salient design insights". Both the CHI and the IS literature use this
>>> term. I am pulling together examples of past papers that do this. He thinks
>>> it could simply be focused on insights about action design research,
>>> although we can also offer other ideas also.
>>> 4. Shaila Miranda is also helping with the senior editing of our paper
>>> and will be the one reviewing our institutional theory. He liked what he
>>> told us, but said she will be the judge.
>>>
>>> We have a few things to do.
>>> 1. Integrate in the gamma interview from today. Ahmed said we can treat
>>> the gamma as the final artifact. Quotes from that interview might be key.
>>> Just a table with the quotes. I think we could also include the beta
>>> quotes, as the beta and gamma are fairly similar. We can use the
>>> applicability test concept to handle/frame the table.
>>> 2. Shiyan is working on the prompt changes from Beta to Gamma.
>>> 3. I will try to converge the ending on salient design insights once I
>>> understand the terminology.
>>> 4. We do want to make sure the institutional stuff is strong. We
>>> probably should look at Shaia's papers to figure out what flavor of
>>> institutional scholar she is. Jocelyn, I think you can help with this, as
>>> well as in making sure our descriptions of journalism/newsrooms are
>>> accurate.
>>> 5. We will need a draft that is 55 pages and can be read by humans. We
>>> have the first part but the second part will take some work.
>>> 6. We have a draft of the reply to reviewers. That may need to change as
>>> we change some of the things above.
>>>
>>>
>>>
>>>
>>>

emails.subject: Fwd: Save the Date - Sept. 12th: Sports AI Innovation Symposium @ Columbia
emails.body:
---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Eddie Mandhry <em3986@columbia.edu>
Date: Mon, Aug 4, 2025 at 11:49 AM
Subject: Save the Date - Sept. 12th: Sports AI Innovation Symposium @
Columbia
To: Eddie Mandhry <em3986@columbia.edu>

Greetings,
I hope this finds you well. I’m writing with a quick note to kindly ask you
to *save the date* for the *2025 Columbia–Dream Sports AI Innovation
Symposium*, which will take place on *Friday, September 12, from 9:00 AM to
4:00 PM* at *Pulitzer Hall, Columbia University*.

This year’s program will feature *keynote talks*, *industry fireside chats*,
and a select number of *lightning talks *showcasing research supported by
the Center.

*Ask:*
If you’re available to attend, we’d love to have you, or members of your
team join us for all or part of the convening. And if you’re potentially
interested in presenting your project during one of the lightning talk
sessions, please let me know. We’re still finalizing the program and
gauging presenter availability, so it would be helpful to know if you’d be
open to being included in a lighting round talk.

You’ll also receive a calendar invite shortly to help block the time.

More details will follow soon. Thanks again for being part of this growing
community.

Warmly,
Eddie
__
Eddie Mandhry
Managing Director
Columbia Dream Sports AI Innovation Center

emails.snippet: FYI ----- Forwarded Message ----- From: Apple <no_reply@ email. apple. com> To: "sreedhar_sivakumaran@ yahoo. com" <sreedhar_sivakumaran@ yahoo. com> Sent: Friday, September 5,

emails.subject: Readback from call
emails.body:
Actually two calls.

Lydia, the first hour with Alex this morning reviewing the gamma seemed
great: how much longer did the call go? Can you share the recording? We can
talk about it tomorrow in your meeting.

Shiyan and I met with the MISQ special issue Senior Editor also this
morning.
He was positive on our approach. We learned a few things:
1. He will add another reviewer who focuses on Action Design Research. That
person did influence the revision plan the senior editor sent us. He may
drop one of the reviewers so we don't end up with four reviewers.
2. He pointed us toward literature in the information systems field that
does applicability tests. He said our interviews probably constitute such
tests. Shiyan is pulling together those papers to see how to use the term.
3. He thinks that the paper could be oriented to in the end provide
"salient design insights". Both the CHI and the IS literature use this
term. I am pulling together examples of past papers that do this. He thinks
it could simply be focused on insights about action design research,
although we can also offer other ideas also.
4. Shaila Miranda is also helping with the senior editing of our paper and
will be the one reviewing our institutional theory. He liked what he told
us, but said she will be the judge.

We have a few things to do.
1. Integrate in the gamma interview from today. Ahmed said we can treat the
gamma as the final artifact. Quotes from that interview might be key. Just
a table with the quotes. I think we could also include the beta quotes, as
the beta and gamma are fairly similar. We can use the applicability test
concept to handle/frame the table.
2. Shiyan is working on the prompt changes from Beta to Gamma.
3. I will try to converge the ending on salient design insights once I
understand the terminology.
4. We do want to make sure the institutional stuff is strong. We probably
should look at Shaia's papers to figure out what flavor of institutional
scholar she is. Jocelyn, I think you can help with this, as well as in
making sure our descriptions of journalism/newsrooms are
accurate.
5. We will need a draft that is 55 pages and can be read by humans. We have
the first part but the second part will take some work.
6. We have a draft of the reply to reviewers. That may need to change as we
change some of the things above.

emails.subject: Resident Memo – Community Guidelines & Building Security
emails.body:
Dear Residents,

Management's  committed  to maintaining a safe and comfortable living
environment. Residents are urged to follow these important guidelines:

   - *Be mindful of noise levels*—please refrain from playing loud music or
   TV between 9:30 PM and 9:00 AM
   - *Clean up after yourself* in all shared spaces, including hallways,
   staircases, and laundry rooms.
   - *Dispose of garbage and recycling properly*—please use designated bins
   and avoid leaving trash in hallways or common areas.
   - *Smoking is strictly prohibited anywhere on the premises.* Violations
   will result in a *$250 fine.*
   - *Do not buzz in anyone you do not personally know.* Unauthorized entry
   compromises the safety of the entire building.
   - *Ensure the front door fully closes and locks behind you* when
   entering or exiting the building.
   - *If you notice someone inside the building who appears unauthorized*,
   report it to management immediately.
   - *Package safety:* If you are expecting a delivery, *retrieve it as
   soon as possible* to help prevent theft. If your building has a
   designated package room, please ensure the door is securely closed after
   use.

We appreciate your cooperation in keeping our community safe and secure.
If you have any questions or concerns, please feel free to reach out.

Best Regards,
MD Squared Property Group

emails.subject: Student Affairs Committee Welcome Back to Campus
emails.body:
Dear Members of the Columbia Student Body,

As the Student Affairs Committee of the University Senate, we would like to
extend a warm welcome back to campus for the fall semester. We hope you had
a restful and enjoyable summer, and if you were traveling, a pleasant trip
back to New York City.

For those unfamiliar with the University Senate, we are the only
democratically elected, university-wide governing body, representing
students, faculty, staff, research officers, administrators, and more. Of
the 111 Senators, there are 25 students who represent all of Columbia’s
schools and affiliate institutions. All student senators are members of the
Student Affairs Committee (SAC), and can be located through the SAC
directory.

The University Senate is a central policy-making body of Columbia
University, and our role is essential to ensuring an effective shared
governance at Columbia University alongside University administration and
the Board of Trustees. This year, we are especially dedicated to
representing a diverse group of voices so as to improve student quality of
life and to promote a collaborative learning and research environment for
the Columbia community.

To this end, we have been working throughout the summer to curate a select
list of priorities. We are committed to initiatives promoting the following
themes:

   1. Affordability
   2. Physical and mental wellness
   3. Common ground amongst student voices and partnership with student
   leaders across Columbia
   4. Transparency and fairness from all members of the shared governance
   structure

We are excited to share more specifics on our initiatives with you in the
coming months, which we believe will have a direct impact on your everyday
lives as Columbia students. We are also committed to hosting town hall
events as we did in the Spring of 2025, and will circulate a schedule in
the coming weeks. Please keep an eye out for future communications, as well
as communications from your elected student Senator.

In the meantime, please do not hesitate to reach out to
SACUniSenate@Columbia.edu with any questions or concerns you have about
student life. We are more than willing to bring your concerns forward to
Columbia’s administration.

We wish you all the best for your first week of classes!

Respectfully,

Your Student Affairs Committee Co-Chairs
Matthew Beck (SEAS)
Huda Paracha (Barnard)
Helen Han Wei Luo (GSAS)

emails.body:
great! I just ping Alex :)

On Sun, Aug 31, 2025 at 12:59 AM Jeff Nickerson <jvnickerson@gmail.com>
wrote:

> Thank you Jocelyn. Your perspective helps a lot. I just finished a game
> night with Alex; we field tested a game he is working on about asymmetric
> information. Lydia, he said he would get back to you; he can meet this
> coming week. The delay:
> ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerStart
> This Message Is From an External Sender
> This message came from outside your organization.
>
> ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerEnd
> Thank you Jocelyn. Your perspective helps a lot.
>
>  I just  finished a game night with Alex; we field tested a game he is
> working on about asymmetric information.
>
> Lydia, he said he would get back to you; he can meet this coming week. The
> delay: they were working on a story about the fed. I think you can ping him
> and he will respond now.
>
> On Sat, Aug 30, 2025 at 11:45 PM Jocelyn McKinnon-Crowley <
> jmckin02@syr.edu> wrote:
>
>> Hi all,
>>
>> I go back into the field tomorrow for dissertation research. I typed up
>> my notes from the conversations with Alex here
>> <https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Lj7Oxbq16BEivBgNSkjMLpeEBQqMIFuHqClZoqEJzPE/edit?usp=sharing >.
>> And I made some comments (probably too many)  in the document as well as
>> adding some vignettes. I can jump back in for edits or to watch more
>> recordings, but probably not until late next week.
>>
>> Best,
>> Jocelyn
>>
>> --
>>
>> *From: *Jeff Nickerson <jvnickerson@gmail.com>
>> *Date: *Tuesday, August 26, 2025 at 3:13 PM
>> *To: *Lydia Chilton <chilton@cs.columbia.edu>, Jocelyn McKinnon-Crowley <
>> jmckin02@syr.edu>, Zhang, Shiyan <shiyan.zhang@lancaster.ac.uk>, Karthik
>> Sreedhar <ks4190@columbia.edu>, Alice Cai <caia@mit.edu>
>> *Subject: *Evaluation for the paper
>>
>> We only have a couple of weeks before the deadline, and so only certain
>> things are possible. Still, it might make sense to think about how we can
>> have more not less evaluation.
>>
>> Since the story is about action design research, the more we can focus on
>> one case the better. But the podcast case so far only involves one person.
>> Here is my sense of things maybe we can do.
>>
>> 1. We are looking to do one more session with the Executive Producer.
>> Lydia, has he gotten back to you? I see him socially Saturday, so let me
>> know if we have something scheduled or what the back and forth has been.
>> 1b. We might want to ask him if we can share the prototype with one
>> of his writers. If we did something with a writer, that might give us
>> another organizational perspective.
>>
>> 2. We have the notes on the one session with the Managing Editor. Could
>> we do another one with him before the deadline? And/or someone underneath
>> the managing editor?
>>
>> This is not our primary case, but we could call it a parallel case, and
>> it raises print vs. broadcast journalism contexts.
>>
>> 3. Could we send out links to the prototype to a bunch of
>> journalists/journalism students along with an open-ended set of questions?
>>
>> I am not sure what we would ask, but I think we would like examples of
>> if/how the prototype helped find something new.
>>
>> 4. Are there some a/b tests or analyses we could do?
>> For example, turn on/off features at certain points in the interaction?
>> Compare to just chatgpt?
>> plot the conversations the participants have?
>>
>> Could we look to see what kind of personae get created and how?
>>
>> Along these lines, could we have stories with holes and see how long it
>> takes for them to get plugged in different configurations of the prototype?
>>
>> 5. Thinking out loud...
>> Anybody teaching journalists this semester? Could we get use data from an
>> assignment or an in class demo?
>>
>>
>>
>>

emails.subject: Columbia CDL HCI-AI Research Interest
emails.body:
Hi Karthik,

I hope this email finds you well. My name is Humad Bari and I am a first-year junior transfer student at UCLA, intending to declare a major in Linguistics & Computer Science. I was fascinated by your paper Simulating Cooperative Prosocial Behavior with Multi-Agent LLMs: Evidence and Mechanisms for AI Agents to Inform Policy Decisions, and how it leverages multi-agent LLM systems to replicate human prosocial behavior and explore unbounded actions to inform policy design. My academic goal is to pursue a Ph.D. in Human-Computer Interaction, focusing on human-AI, social media, and behavioral interaction. I was wondering if there might be any way I could volunteer towards your research, as I deeply admire the HCI work you’re doing.
This past summer, I co-authored a Human-AI interaction paper under review at ACM CHI 2026 with Professor Anthony Chen at UCLA, analyzing UX professionals’ interactions with GenAI tools. I am eager to apply my experience with human-AI research to design and evaluate human-robot interactions. I have beginner-to-intermediate experience with Python programming, a solid foundation in statistical analysis (including ANOVA and t-tests), and a strong understanding of user-centered design principles. I also have very proficient experience applying thematic analysis and other qualitative research methods to examine user behavioral patterns.
I would be grateful for the opportunity to voluntarily assist in your research in any capacity. I have attached my resume for your consideration and would be happy to provide any additional information.
Thank you very much for your time.
Best regards,
Humad Bari
hbari1@ucla.edu
(949) 278-4930

emails.subject: ​Subject: Beware of Email and Social Engineering Text Scams
emails.body:
[image: image.png]

Subject: Beware of Email and Social Engineering Text Scams

Dear Students, Faculty, and Staff,

With the start of the Fall term, Cybersecurity and Email Systems are
experiencing a surge of sophisticated phishing and social engineering
attempts. They are targeting our community with fake job offers of remote
internships and quick cash. These messages may look legitimate, but are
designed to steal money, personal information, and University credentials.

Current threat example:

   -

   Scammers are impersonating Columbia professors and offering fake
   Internships via email. After initial email communication, they move to
   text messaging and conduct fake interviews to gain trust. Next, they
   request personal information to set you up in the Columbia payroll system,
   followed by sending a legitimate looking $2500 check to deposit for office
   supplies. By the time your bank processes it, and realizes it's fake, it’s
   too late, and your provided information is compromised.

Common scams aimed at college students

   -

   Fake scholarship / financial-aid offers
   -

      Scammer emails or texts: “You’ve been awarded a scholarship/grant —
      just pay a small processing fee or click here to claim.”
      <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://financialwellness.utah.edu/blog/posts/2024/scams.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!WR3NSy3m5sYvYizBK1dAiPvVHYj2A8dguDbpChV95CIK6ul_KZu_vrFGpoMT9SGeHYWHefAvLpQylf5w7JYYPfQ0XNh9ozhMMg$ >
      -

      Red flags: unsolicited, “guaranteed” award, asks for payment or
      bank/SSN info to “release funds”.
      <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.needhambank.com/resources/a-guide-for-college-students-be-aware-of-these-4-scams/?utm_source=chatgpt.com__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!WR3NSy3m5sYvYizBK1dAiPvVHYj2A8dguDbpChV95CIK6ul_KZu_vrFGpoMT9SGeHYWHefAvLpQylf5w7JYYPfQ0XNgKUPJmqA$ >Why
      students are targeted: they often seek financial help, and may be less
      risk-averse.

      -

   Fake job / remote work / “easy money” offers
   -

      Example: “Work from home, earn $2,000/week, just do data entry for
      us.”
      -

      Often involves sending/receiving checks, or providing bank details,
      or clicking links that lead to credential phishing.
      -

      Students doing part-time jobs or looking for flexibility are
      especially vulnerable.

      -

   Phishing emails targeting student accounts/university systems
   -

      Emails that appear to be from the university IT department: “Your
      student account will be suspended unless you verify your
credentials here.”
      -

      Links lead to fake login pages, credentials stolen, and campus
      systems compromised.
      -

   Fake apartment/moving/textbook deals
   -

      Scammers post appealing housing listings (“cheap, near campus”),
      request a deposit or personal info up front.
      -

      Fake textbook websites or sellers: you pay, but don’t receive the
      book (or give your payment/credit-card info).
      -

   Social-media / influencer/modeling “offers”
   -

      On Instagram/TikTok: “We want you to be our model/influencer. Send
      your banking info for it, or deposit a check, then transfer part back.”

      <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.uwcu.org/loans/articles/5-financial-scams-college-students?utm_source=chatgpt.com__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!WR3NSy3m5sYvYizBK1dAiPvVHYj2A8dguDbpChV95CIK6ul_KZu_vrFGpoMT9SGeHYWHefAvLpQylf5w7JYYPfQ0XNgbGWv7cg$ >
      -

      Young adults are big consumers of social media, making them targets
      for these more “glamorous” scams.

      -

   Debt-relief / credit-repair lies
   -

      Students may already have loans, credit cards; scammers promise to
      “erase” debt or improve credit for a fee.

      -

   Fake or urgent billing/tuition notices
   -

      Email or text claiming the student owes tuition or fees, forcing them
      to click a link or pay immediately.

   -

   Smishing (SMS) or WhatsApp hooks
   -

      Texts: “Your package is held for customs, click link to verify” or
      “Your account has been locked, verify here.” Students often switch to
      mobile early, so this is used.
      -

   Deepfake/spoofing / “imposter” voice or video scams
   -

      Some reports: scammers using a fake voice or spoofed university phone
      numbers to trick students into handing over info or paying.
      <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://www.websterbank.com/resources/security/article/dont-get-schooled-by-scammers-how-to-spot-common-student-scams/?utm_source=chatgpt.com__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!WR3NSy3m5sYvYizBK1dAiPvVHYj2A8dguDbpChV95CIK6ul_KZu_vrFGpoMT9SGeHYWHefAvLpQylf5w7JYYPfQ0XNi09jzvzA$ >

Remember:

   -

   No legitimate employer will ask you to pay for materials or
   certifications to start a job.

   -

   University officials will not use personal Email accounts for official
   business, job offers, or payment requests.

How to protect yourself:

   -

   Do not share personal information (SSN, bank details, IDs) via email or
   text.

   -

   Verify requests using known contact information; avoid using phone
   numbers or links from suspicious messages.

   -

   Be cautious with links and attachments. Hover to preview links; if in
   doubt, don’t click or open.

   -

   Check the sender’s address and reply‑to. Watch for mismatched names and
   domains.

   -

   Treat urgent or secret payment requests—especially involving gift cards
   or payroll changes—as suspicious.

If you receive a suspicious message:

   -

   Do not reply, click links, or open attachments.

   -

   Report it by forwarding the message with full headers to
   phishing@columbia.edu. CUIMC users: spam-abuse@cumc.columbia.edu.

   -

   If in doubt, contact the CUIT Service Desk at 212-854-1919 (CUIMC:
   212-305-4357, option 5).

If you clicked a link or entered information:

   -

   Change your password immediately at Manage My UNI (CUIMC: Self Service
   Password Reset [SSPR]).

   -

   Contact the CUIT Service Desk at 212-854-1919 (CUIMC: 212-305-4357,
   option 5).

   -

   If you have sent money or gift card codes, contact your bank or card
   issuer immediately and notify Public Safety (publicsafety@columbia.edu).

Finally, CUIT thanks you for your support and effort in helping us keep
Columbia safe.

Jose Santiago

AVP of Client Support Services

Columbia University Information Technology

Medha Bhalodkar

CISO and AVP, Enterprise Risk Management

Columbia University Information Technology

Thanks,
Chuck Eigen, PMP, ITIL
Project Director Portfolio Management Office
Security and Identity Access Management Portfolio Director
Columbia University Information Technology
Cell:  646.842.0903
Office: 212.851.2912

emails.subject: Fw: Scholarship Opportunities for Engineering Students
emails.body:
----- Forwarded Message ----- From: Jayanthi subramanian <jayanthi@stanford.edu>To: "students@cs.stanford.edu" <students@cs.stanford.edu>Sent: Monday, October 27, 2025 at 03:19:13 PM PDTSubject: Scholarship Opportunities for Engineering Students
    
I’m reaching out on behalf of the American Council of Engineering Companies (ACEC) Research Institute to share several scholarship opportunities available to qualified undergraduate and graduate engineering students for the 2026–2027 academic year.
 
 We would greatly appreciate your help in sharing this information with your students. By spreading the word, you can help ensure more aspiring engineers have access to financial support that can make a meaningful difference in their education and professional development.
 
 The application deadline for most scholarships is March 13, 2026.
 
 Please find attached a promotional graphic and suggested language you can include in your communications with students
    
 The ACEC Research Institute is the leading source of knowledge and thought leadership dedicated to creating a more sustainable, safe, secure, and technically advanced built environment. Through research, forecasts, trend analysis, and academic scholarships, the Institute provides insights and strategies that strengthen and elevate the engineering profession.
 
 Through the ACEC Research Institute’s online scholarship portal, qualified students can access more than 100 scholarships totaling nearly $1 million in awards. These include a $5,000 scholarship specifically designated for students from California, as well as merit and need-based opportunities.
 
 Students complete one application through the portal, which will automatically match them with all scholarships for which they qualify. Application deadlines vary, so students are encouraged to apply as early as possible.
 
 https://urldefense.com/v3/__https://acecinstitute.awardspring.com/__;!!BDUfV1Et5lrpZQ!TRP0F2n0_Reept3Q0xJjZwctt9bo9c4WwVSXMAtw7ajqobOE4PBkTRvxeg_yOIfRZ9hBlh7WYgAYA45ipNqOQHpgbke0HqmY$ 
    
 If you have any questions or need additional information about these scholarships or other Institute initiatives, please don’t hesitate to contact me. Thank you for helping us reach and support the next generation of engineering leaders!
 
 Mira Schueler
 
 Manager, Engagement
 
 ACEC Research Institute
 
 Office: 202-347-7474
 
 Mobile: 202-682-4304
 
 1400 L Street NW, Suite 400
 
 Washington, DC 20005-3529
 
 Email: mschueler@acec.org

Volo Team

emails.body:
Dear Tenants, This is a general reminder to all tenants that rent is due on the 1st of each month. We kindly ask you to ensure that your rent payment is submitted on time. Timely payments are essential for maintaining the smooth operation of
ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerStart
This Message Is From an External Sender
This message came from outside your organization.
 
ZjQcmQRYFpfptBannerEnd
Dear Tenants,

This is a general reminder to all tenants that rent is due on the 1st of each month. We kindly ask you to ensure that your rent payment is submitted on time. Timely payments are essential for maintaining the smooth operation of the building and ensuring all services and facilities continue to function effectively.

Please also remember to double-check that your Water/Sewer Fee is paid in full alongside your rent.

If payment is not received by the 5th of the month, a late fee will be applied to your account. To avoid this fee, we strongly encourage you to submit your payment today or tomorrow if you have not already done so.

Your prompt attention to this matter is greatly appreciated. Thank you for fulfilling this important responsibility.

Best Regards,
MD Squared Property Group
